title: Lobster roll
subtitle: Cold lobster in mayonnaise on a buttered, toasted split-top roll
excerpt: The Maine version: cold steamed lobster dressed simply in mayonnaise with a little celery and lemon, served in a buttered and toasted split-top roll. There are two schools (cold-mayonnaise and warm-butter); this is the cold version. The lobster is the whole point, keep everything else minimal.
sourceNotes: The lobster roll is a staple of New England seafood culture, particularly associated with the coastal towns of Maine, Connecticut, and Massachusetts. The cold-mayonnaise Maine version and the warm-butter Connecticut version represent two distinct regional traditions. The dish became widely known outside New England during the mid-to-late twentieth century. American fishing and cooking traditions from coastal New England are documented in the USDA Agricultural Extension publications from the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

content.text: The Maine lobster roll is one of the most constrained dishes in American cooking: lobster, mayonnaise, a little celery, lemon, a split-top roll. The constraints are the point. Any addition (avocado, herbs, aioli, tarragon, shallots) nudges it toward something more elaborate and loses what makes the original good: the sweetness of properly cooked lobster, lightly dressed, with nothing between you and it.

The lobster should be steamed, not boiled (steaming produces a less waterlogged claw), and fully cooled before dressing. The mayonnaise should be kept to just enough to coat, the lobster should not be swimming in it. The roll is a split-top hot-dog-style bun, buttered on the flat sides and toasted in a hot pan until golden. The contrast between the warm toasted bread and the cold filling is half the dish.

items.name: Lobster
items.prepNote: live, about 500 to 600 g each, steamed and meat removed

items.name: Bread
items.prepNote: split-top hot-dog rolls or brioche buns

content.text: Bring 5 cm of salted water to a rolling boil in a stockpot fitted with a steamer basket. Place the lobsters in the basket, cover tightly, and steam for 12 to 14 minutes for a 500 g lobster. Remove and cool completely, either at room temperature for 20 minutes or in a bowl of cold water for 10. Crack the claws and tail, remove all the meat, and cut into rough 2 cm pieces. Check for any shell fragments.

Combine the lobster pieces, the finely sliced celery, {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, and a squeeze of lemon juice in a bowl. Season with a little salt and white pepper. Toss gently. The mixture should be lightly coated, not wet. Taste and adjust the lemon. Keep cold until the rolls are ready.

Spread the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter on the flat sides of each roll. Toast butter-side down in a frying pan over medium heat for 1 to 2 minutes until golden. Spoon the cold lobster filling generously into each warm roll. Scatter chives over if using. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Steam a 500 g lobster for no more than 12 minutes; the tail meat should be just opaque and firm, not springy when pressed
items.cause: Overcooked; lobster goes from perfect to overdone quickly

items.fix: Pat the lobster pieces dry after removing from the shell; use only enough mayonnaise to coat lightly
items.cause: Lobster not properly cooled and drained before dressing, or too much mayonnaise
items.symptom: Filling is too wet and dresses in liquid

items.fix: Dress the lobster ahead but only fill the rolls at the moment of serving; the warm roll + cold filling contrast is essential
items.cause: Filling added to the roll too far in advance

items.fix: Season the filling with salt and white pepper. Add lemon juice freely; lobster is better with acid.
items.cause: Not enough lemon or salt in the dressing

content.text: The lobster roll is a New England summer staple. Roadside shacks along the Maine and Connecticut coasts sell it from June to September. The two versions are cold Maine-style and warm Connecticut-style, butter only and no mayo. Each is a different way of eating the same thing, and loyalties run deep. The cold version travels well and holds up on the way to the beach. The warm version is best straight away. Both joined the American seafood canon in the early twentieth century. Lobster had been treated as a poverty food until coastal fishing towns saw that city buyers would pay for what they had been eating for years.
